topic: You Don't Need to Watch Charts All Day — Build Rules, Not Screen Time

x.text:
Most people think trading means staring at charts 8 hours a day.

I backtested a strategy that checks ONE thing at market close each day. Takes 2 minutes.

10 years of SPY data. Beat the guy glued to his screen.

Your strategy should work harder than you do.

#Trading #SideHustle
x.image_text:
2 MINUTES A DAY
BEAT 8 HOURS
OF SCREEN TIME

linkedin.text:
"I'd love to trade but I have a full-time job."

I hear this every single week.

Here's what most people don't realize: the best strategies I've backtested don't require you to watch a single candle during market hours.

I compared two approaches on 10 years of SPY data:

Approach A: An end-of-day strategy that checks 3 conditions at market close. If all 3 are true, it enters a position. Takes about 2 minutes.

Approach B: An intraday strategy that requires monitoring 5-minute charts during market hours. Needs 4-6 hours of active screen time.

Results:
— End-of-day: 58% win rate, 1.8R avg winner, 43% total return
— Intraday: 52% win rate, 1.4R avg winner, 31% total return

The end-of-day strategy won. With 2 minutes of work per day.

Why? Fewer trades = fewer mistakes. No emotional decisions at 10:30 AM when the chart does something scary. No overtrading because you're bored. Just: check conditions, execute or don't, close the laptop.

The biggest lie in trading is that more screen time = more money.

The people building real second incomes from the market aren't glued to Bloomberg terminals. They built simple rule-based systems and spend their days doing literally anything else.

Your strategy should work harder than you do.

Could you commit to 2 minutes a day after market close?

#Trading #SideHustle #PassiveIncome #FinancialFreedom #StockMarket
linkedin.image_text:
END-OF-DAY: 43% RETURN
INTRADAY: 31% RETURN

2 MINUTES BEAT 6 HOURS

facebook.text:
Raise your hand if you think trading means sitting at a computer all day watching squiggly lines.

I used to think that too. Then I ran the numbers.

I backtested two approaches over 10 years of SPY data. One required checking the chart once at market close (2 minutes). The other required watching 5-minute charts for hours.

The 2-minute-a-day approach actually performed BETTER.

58% win rate vs. 52%. Higher average winner. Less stress. And you can do it after dinner while watching Netflix.

The secret is building rules instead of building screen time. Write down exactly when you buy and when you sell. Then just follow the rules once a day.

No staring at charts. No gut feelings. No panic selling at 10 AM.

Who else thought trading had to be a full-time thing?
facebook.image_text:
TRADING DOESN'T MEAN
STARING AT SCREENS ALL DAY

2 minutes after market close.
That's it.

content.instagram:
"I don't have time to trade."

You have 2 minutes after dinner? Then you have time.

Here's what I found when I backtested two approaches on 10 years of SPY data:

Approach 1: Check 3 simple conditions at market close each day. Takes 2 minutes. Enter a position if all 3 are met.

Approach 2: Watch 5-minute charts during market hours. Requires 4-6 hours of screen time.

Results:
End-of-day (2 min/day): 58% win rate, 43% total return
Intraday (6 hrs/day): 52% win rate, 31% total return

The lazy approach won.

Here's the trick: build RULES, not screen time.

Write down exactly 3 things that need to be true before you buy. Example:
1. SPY is above its 50-day moving average (trend is up)
2. RSI (momentum score) is below 40 (short-term pullback)
3. Today's candle closed green (buyers stepping back in)

All 3 true at close? Buy. Otherwise, do nothing.

That's it. No watching charts during work. No panic selling. No "gut feeling" trades.

The best traders I've studied don't work harder. They built systems that work FOR them.

.
.
.
#trading #stockmarket #sidehustle #passiveincome #financialfreedom #investing #money #tradingstrategy #SPY #endofday #rulesbasedtrading #beginnerinvestor #wealthbuilding #tradingtips #automation #smartmoney

reddit.title: Backtested end-of-day vs. intraday strategies on 10 years of SPY — the 2-minute daily check won
reddit.body:
I've been comparing time commitment vs. returns across different strategy types and wanted to share results that surprised me.

Setup: 10 years of SPY daily and 5-minute data. Two strategies with similar logic (mean reversion entries with trend filter), but different timeframes:

**End-of-day strategy**: Checks 3 conditions at market close each day (price above 50 DMA, RSI-14 below 40, bullish close). If all true, enters at next open. Fixed 2R target, 1 ATR stop. Takes about 2 minutes per day.

**Intraday strategy**: Same core logic applied to 5-minute bars during market hours. Requires active monitoring for entries and exits. Roughly 4-6 hours of screen time on active days.

Results:
- End-of-day: 58% win rate, 1.8R avg winner, ~43% total return, 127 trades total
- Intraday: 52% win rate, 1.4R avg winner, ~31% total return, 890 trades total

The end-of-day strategy had fewer trades, better win rate, and higher per-trade expectancy. The intraday strategy generated more trades but with lower quality and higher commission drag.

My hypothesis on why: the daily timeframe filters out a ton of noise. Intraday charts are full of false signals, and the more decisions you make per day, the more opportunities for emotional or impulsive trades.

Caveats:
- This is SPY-specific. Results would likely differ for individual stocks or other instruments
- The intraday strategy might perform better with more sophisticated execution rules
- Didn't account for psychological factors (decision fatigue, stress), which would favor the end-of-day approach even more
- Commission costs were estimated at $1/trade and hurt the intraday strategy disproportionately

For anyone with a day job exploring trading as a side pursuit, end-of-day strategies seem significantly underrated. The time-adjusted returns aren't even close.

Anyone else running end-of-day systems? Curious what conditions others are using.

tiktok.caption: you don't need 8 hours of screen time to trade. I backtested a 2-minute daily strategy and it beat the all-day approach. build rules, not screen time. #trading #stockmarket #money #sidehustle #financialfreedom #passiveincome
